content.full_text:
RAvI MEHTA
CHAIRMAN
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
August 4, 1997
Ms. Joyce Hicks
Assistant City Attorney
City of Oakland
One City Hall Plaza, 6th Floor
Oakland, California 94612
Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-97-334
Dear Ms. Hicks:
This letter is a response to your request for advice regarding the provisions of the
Political Reform Act (the “Act”).’
QUESTION
May Councilmember Russo reopen his prior campaign committee for the purpose of
spending leftover funds from his pre-January 1, 1997, officeholder account for officeholder
expenses?
CONCLUSION
Yes, Councilmember Russo may reopen his prior campaign committee for the purpose of
spending leftover funds from his pre-January 1, 1997, officeholder account for officeholder
expenses.
FACTS
You are requesting advice on behalf of Oakland City Councilmember John Russo.
Oakland adopted its Campaign Finance Reform Act, Ordinance number 11612 C.M.S, on July
20, 1993; it became effective January 1, 1994. One of its provisions, section 310, provides for
the establishment of an officeholder account by city officeholders. The annual contributions that
may be received by a district city councilmember may not exceed $25,000. Councilmember
Government Code sections 81000
- 91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109
-
18995, of the California Code of Regulations.
428 J STREET, SUITE 800, SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814
(916’ 322-5660


File No. A-97-334
Page 2
Russo is a district city councilmember. Effective January 1, 1997, the Oakland City Council
suspended the Campaign Finance Reform Ordinance due to conflicts between its provisions and
Proposition 208.
Councilmember Russo closed his prior campaign committee through which he received
contributions for his pre-Proposition 208 officeholder account and transferred amounts that did
not exceed $10,000 into a Proposition 208 officeholder account, under the beliefthat the Political
Reform Act required him to do so. He was not required to do so under Oakland’s ordinance. A
surplus remains from Councilmember Russo’s officeholder account.
ANALYSIS
Assuming Councilmember Russo has transferred $10,000 — the maximum amount for
1997 — into his Proposition 208 officeholder account, he may not transfer any additional funds
or accept any further contributions for this account in 1997. (Section 853 13(a).) However, you
ask whether Councilmember Russo may continue to spend funds left over from his pre
Proposition 208 officeholder account. These funds were raised prior to January 1, 1997.
Regulation 18530.1 states that funds raised prior to the effective date ofProposition 208 and held
in a controlled committee established pursuant to section 84101 prior to January 1, 1997, can be
carried over and spent for any purpose, consistent with the personal use provisions of sections
895 10-895 18.
Moreover, the Avila Advice Letter, No. A-96-355, addressed whether elected city
officials in Los Angeles were required to close their city officeholder accounts that contain funds
raised prior to the effective date of Proposition 208. We advised as follows:
“Consistent with the Commission’s regulations and advice
concerning the use of ‘old money’ under Proposition 208, elected
city officials in Los Angeles are not required to close their city
officeholder accounts or dispose of the funds in such accounts.
City officials may keep their officeholder accounts and spend the
funds in those accounts consistent with sections 89510-89518
governing the use of campaign funds and the city law and
regulations concerning officeholder accounts. Effective January 1,
1997, however, city officials may not raise additional funds in
these accounts.”
Under the facts presented, Councilmember Russo may reopen his prior campaign committee for
the purpose of spending leftover funds from his pre-Proposition 208 officeholder account for
officeholder expenses. However, any new funds must be raised into an account designated for a
future election under the contribution limits ofProposition 208, or in a Proposition 208
officeholder account pursuant to the $250 per contributor and the $10,000 per year aggregate
limits.


File No. A-97-334
Page 3
If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-
5660.
Sincerely,
Steven G. Churchwell
General Counsel
F,
-
By:
Hyla P. Wagner
Staff Counsel, Legal Division
SGC:HW:tls

QUESTION: May Councilmember Russo reopen his prior campaign committee for the purpose of
spending leftover funds from his pre-January 1, 1997, officeholder account for officeholder
expenses?

CONCLUSION: Yes, Councilmember Russo may reopen his prior campaign committee for the purpose of
spending leftover funds from his pre-January 1, 1997, officeholder account for officeholder
expenses.
